did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (specifically ResNet-50) for melanoma classification in dermoscopic images. It employed proportional morphological dilation as a preprocessing technique to enhance the context for the deep learning classifier, evaluating performance on the ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2018 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel approach called ProMorph for melanoma classification. It details specific methods (proportional morphological dilation, ResNet-50), describes the use of ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2018 datasets, and reports new quantitative results (AUC, Recall, F1 scores) comparing the proposed method against baselines and previous studies. It is published as a conference paper in ICIAI 2024.

title: ProMorph: Melanoma Classification in Dermoscopic Images using Proportional Morphological Dilation and Convolutional Neural Networks
journal: 2024 the 8th International Conference on Innovation in Artificial Intelligence (ICIAI 2024)
brief_summary: This study introduces ProMorph, a novel approach for melanoma classification in dermoscopic images that employs proportional morphological dilation to adaptively enlarge segmentation masks based on lesion size. The method utilizes a ResNet-50 convolutional neural network classifier and is evaluated on the ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2018 datasets. Performance is compared against absolute dilation methods and models without dilation to assess the impact of the proposed preprocessing technique. ProMorph achieved an AUC of 0.8029 on ISIC 2016 and 0.8988 on ISIC 2018, outperforming absolute dilation in most metrics. Notably, the model demonstrated superior recall on the ISIC 2016 dataset compared to previous studies, highlighting its efficacy in capturing melanoma cases.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for classification in both ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2018 datasets. In ISIC 2016, it is labeled as 'Malignant'. In ISIC 2018, it is one of 7 classes but combined with non-melanoma classes for binary classification.
The negative class in the binary melanoma classification task. In ISIC 2016, labeled as 'Benign (Non Melanoma)'. In ISIC 2018, all non-melanoma classes (6 classes) were combined into one class.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between malignant (melanoma) and benign (non-melanoma) skin lesions. For the ISIC 2016 dataset, the classes are explicitly 'Malignant' and 'Benign (Non Melanoma)'. For the ISIC 2018 dataset, six benign classes are combined into one 'non-melanoma' class to create a binary classification task. The model uses a sigmoid activation function for the final dense layer, indicating binary classification probabilities.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016
datasets_used.description: The 2016 International Skin Imaging Collaboration dataset, sourced from the ISIC website. It contains dermoscopic skin lesion images with paired binary masks representing manual skin lesion segmentation by ISIC experts.

use.training: Used for training the ProMorph model (900 images from the training subset).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate validation set; the study notes the dataset is split into training and testing subsets.
use.testing: Used for testing the ProMorph model (279 images from the testing subset).
use.other: Used to compare ProMorph's recall against previous studies.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The 2018 International Skin Imaging Collaboration dataset. It contains dermoscopic images with 7 total classes (6 benign, 1 malignant). For this study, non-melanoma classes were combined into one class. Unlike ISIC 2016, it does not provide segmentation masks for all samples, requiring machine-identified masks via the study's segmentation pipeline.

use.training: Used for training the ProMorph model (10,015 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the ProMorph model (1,512 images).
use.other: Used to evaluate the impact of proportional morphological dilation on machine-identified segmentation masks.

statistical_methods_used.method: Comparison of performance metrics (AUC, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, Specificity, F1 Score)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the proposed ProMorph method against absolute dilation methods and models without dilation on the ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2018 datasets.
To benchmark the recall and other metrics of ProMorph against existing works (e.g., JordanYap, BF-TB, CUMED, GTDL, ThrunLab, Burdick et al.) on the ISIC 2016 dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (AUC, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, Specificity, F1 Score) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2016.notes: ProMorph outperforms Absolute Dilation in AUC, Recall, and F1. ProMorph has the best recall across all previous studies (JordanYap, BF-TB, CUMED, GTDL, ThrunLab, Burdick et al.). ProMorph's AUC (0.803) and Recall (0.787) are higher than Burdick et al. (2017).

ISIC 2018.notes: ProMorph outperforms Absolute Dilation in all metrics other than specificity (where they are tied). ProMorph outperforms the model without dilation in all evaluated metrics, achieving a higher AUC score by approximately 0.019. Machine-identified masks showed exceptional proficiency in AUC, Accuracy, and Specificity.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights (frozen for first 20 epochs, then fine-tuned)

training_details.training_schedule: Pre-trained for 20 epochs with frozen ResNet-50 weights, then fine-tuned for 50 epochs total

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Absolute Dilation (ResNet-50 with Absolute Morphological Dilation)
preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using Black Hat Filter, binary thresholding, and Inpainting
Vignette correction (replacing black corner pixels with white)
Morphological opening (dilation and erosion) to remove small noise from masks
Interquartile Range filtering to remove unusually large/small segmented regions
Proportional Morphological Dilation of segmentation masks (dilation values linearly interpolated between 60 and 90 pixels based on lesion area)
Data augmentation: Rotation, width and height shifts, shear, zoom, and horizontal flip

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to increase sample variability and help the model generalize better.

conclusions: The proposed ProMorph method, which employs proportional morphological dilation based on lesion size, demonstrates a positive impact on improving melanoma classification performance compared to absolute dilation and no dilation methods.
ProMorph outperformed absolute dilation in AUC, Recall, and F1 scores on the ISIC 2016 dataset, and in all metrics except specificity on the ISIC 2018 dataset.
ProMorph achieved an AUC of 0.8029 on ISIC 2016 and 0.8988 on ISIC 2018, surpassing the absolute dilation baseline in both datasets.
ProMorph achieved the highest recall (0.787) among all compared previous studies on the ISIC 2016 dataset, indicating superior capability in capturing melanoma cases.

Machine-identified segmentation masks (used for ISIC 2018) showed exceptional proficiency in AUC, Accuracy, and Specificity, suggesting that automated segmentation can maintain high accuracy and potentially reduce reliance on time-intensive human annotation.

False positives in the ISIC 2016 dataset were attributed to the model's difficulty distinguishing color variations between benign and malignant lesions, while false positives in ISIC 2018 were linked to color changes caused by preprocessing.
False negatives were observed in cases where skin lesions included nails, which hampered the segmentation technique.

Future work may involve integrating the ABCDE Rule into the proportionality stage of the dilation algorithm to allow larger dilation for asymmetrical cases (typically melanoma) and smaller dilation for symmetrical cases (usually benign).
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the ISIC 2016 and ISIC 2018 datasets, which limits the generalizability of the findings to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for performance comparisons, relying only on descriptive statistics.

The model exhibits low precision (0.3758 on ISIC 2016, 0.5427 on ISIC 2018), indicating a high rate of false positives, which is a significant concern for clinical deployment where specificity is critical.

Performance on ISIC 2018 relies on machine-identified segmentation masks rather than ground-truth expert annotations, introducing potential bias from segmentation errors inherent in the automated pipeline.
The dataset is small (1,279 images for ISIC 2016), which may lead to overfitting or limited statistical power despite the use of data augmentation.
The model's performance is sensitive to preprocessing artifacts; false positives were attributed to color changes caused by the vignette correction and grayscale conversion steps.
The segmentation pipeline fails on lesions including nails, leading to false negatives and indicating a limitation in handling complex anatomical contexts.
The study compares against previous works using inconsistent metrics (e.g., Precision and F1 are missing for several baseline models in Table 3), making direct comprehensive comparison difficult.
